narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 60.5% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest thread pulling things toward struggle was your Year 3 decile of 3.0, which stood out as a sign that sustaining top performance might be tough in law school's grind. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 46.0, below the levels that typically light the way to bar success for students like you. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 echoed that early pattern, suggesting the academic climb started rocky and stayed that way. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5 and upper-middle family income, those factors nudged the story further toward hurdles, as they haven't historically shielded others in similar spots from bar stumbles. Your background as a white male pursuing full-time studies layered in subtly, aligning with patterns where focus can waver under pressure. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's this blend painting a picture of resilience tested early and often. The model isn't judging your potential or heart; it's just reading the tea leaves from thousands of journeys like yours. If you boost that LSAT or push your deciles higher next time, it could rewrite the ending entirely. You've got the target set on law—keep fighting for it. This isn't the close of your book; it's just one plot twist. Hang in there.
